flow.name: Article Version Lifecycle
flow.description: Creates a fresh article, publishes it, forks it to produce a new draft, retrieves the article to confirm the draft version exists, deletes the draft version, verifies the deleted version returns 404, then deletes the entire article as teardown — leaving the project in a clean state.

step.name: Create Article
step.notes: The article is created in draft status. project_version_id (ctx.versionId) determines which documentation version the article belongs to. The returned id becomes state.createdArticleId and is used as the article_id path param in all subsequent steps.

step.name: Publish Article
step.notes: version_number must reference a draft version. After this call the article version transitions from draft to published and becomes visible to readers. state.createdVersionNumber (captured in Step 1) is used here without quotes in the JSON body because it is an integer, not a string.

step.name: Fork Published Version (create draft)
step.notes: The fork endpoint has no request body. It creates a new draft version based on the currently published content. Returns 409 Conflict if a draft already exists. state.draftVersionNumber is the new draft's version_number — used in Steps 5 and 6. state.publishedVersionNumber is the still-active published version number (public_version).

step.name: Retrieve Article — Verify Draft Exists
step.notes: GET /articles/{id} returns the latest version (the draft created by the fork). Asserting data.version_number equals state.draftVersionNumber confirms the fork was applied and the article now surfaces the new draft as its current version.

step.name: Delete Draft Version
step.notes: Deletes the draft version created by the fork in Step 3. After deletion the article reverts to showing only the published version (state.publishedVersionNumber). state.deletedVersionNumber is captured from the request path param for use in Step 6.

step.name: Verify Deleted Version Returns 404
step.notes: Confirms the deleted draft version is truly gone. A 200 response here would be a test failure — the version should no longer exist after Step 5.

step.name: Delete Article (cleanup)
step.notes: teardown: true — this step always runs even if Steps 2–6 failed, to ensure the article created in Step 1 is not left orphaned in the project. If Step 1 itself failed and state.createdArticleId is not set, the test implementation should skip this step gracefully.
